```

?? newapp/
```

$ git status --short --untracked-files' will return:
```
 M project/accounts/tests/test_api.py
?? newapp/__init__.py
?? newapp/admin.py
?? newapp/apps.py
?? newapp/migrations/__init__.py
?? newapp/models.py
?? newapp/tests/test_views.py
?? newapp/views.py
```
So that we can just check by the file names. See: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-status/1.8.1#git-status---untracked-filesltmodegt

I'd like to eventually convert this into separate settings, such as:
* exchanges
* projects
* destinations

...with appropriate defaults set for each.

This would mean the config not being quite as configurable (since it would no longer be possible to set a different `projects` filter for each exchange) - however I don't think we ever need anything that complex.

In return, we'd get:
* easier to understand/set config variables (no hard to read JSON blobs in the environment)
* the ability to easily add a project filter without having to copy-paste the list of exchanges

We should also stop setting these environment variables in Heroku, and use the defaults here instead (I don't like hiding settings in Heroku config when they aren't secrets). The environment variables would then only be used in development for testing/filtering etc.

See also [bug 1281821](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1281821).

It is uttermost important to keep this PR backward-compatible, so I did a small study.

I [searched through all *jupyter/ipython* sources for `update_config()` invocations](https://github.com/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=update_config++repo%3Aipython%2Fipython+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fdashboards+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fdocker-stacks+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjupyter+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjupyter_client+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjupyter_console+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjupyter_core+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjupyter_markdown+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjupyterhub+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjupyter-kernels+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjupyter-notebook+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fjvm-repr+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fnbconvert+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fnbdime+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fnbformat+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fnbgrader+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fnbviewer+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fnotebook+repo%3Ajupyter%2Fqt-console+extension%3Apy&type=Code&ref=advsearch&l=&l=)
and I singled out the following x4 (+ x5) `config_update()` usages:
1. [ipython/ipython@IPython/core/application.py#L462: self.update_config(<cmd-line-config>)](/ipython/ipython/blob/2118e16d74a1d4a94d73f4e0f74fbb5d3136297b/IPython/core/application.py#L462) 
   in overridden `Application.initialize()`.
2. [jupyter/jupyter_core@jupyter_core/application.py#L246: self.update_config(<cmd-line-config>)](/jupyter/jupyter_core/blob/59e09ff1c6f94d1ebbe59265af665b46a85cdbe1/jupyter_core/application.py#L246) 
   same as above.
3. [jupyter/notebook@notebook/notebookapp.py#L1091: self.update_config(<file_to_run or notebook_dir>)](/jupyter/notebook/blob/e4524a752bdcbd7083c0ba3039eec033b49e0849/notebook/notebookapp.py#L1091) 
   in overridden `Application.parse_command_line()`.
4. [ipython/ipython@IPython/core/magics/config.py#L156: configurable.update_config(<notebook-locals>)](/ipython/ipython/blob/4950ed10259c7b210cb9620c1881bb39a51ae99f/IPython/core/magics/config.py#L156) 
   in `%config` line-magic (is the "user-provided high priority calls" you talked about @minrk?)
5. x5 more usages in test-cases or other projects (e.g. nbgrader).

Now, for all of them, simply upgrading to this branch of *traitlets* would cause no harm, because:
- no `tag(envvar=...)` would exist in these downstream projects;
- any pre-existing *trait-default from `os.environ`* patterns would continue to work before, and configs would overwrite envvars.

Assuming that you Jupyter-devs want to *really* take advantage of this PR so you start adding tags to various traits.
- Current defaults of respecting envvars would benefit x5 places of (5).
- Of course (4) must NOT respect the environment, but apply strictly all user-defined locals from notebook-cell - so intervention needed.

For the rest, the situation requires intervention **for both cases.**
Specifically:

- for cases (1) & (2), the overridden `Application.initialize()` method, seems "strange",
  since it replicates the logic already present in traitlet's `Application.parse_command_line()`,
  which it already invokes, first thing at the top.
  So maybe this method needs attention anyway, not to duplicate stuff.
  But even if defaults were NOT to respect env, they would require modifications.
 
- case (3) would suffer from current defaults; but in this case i'm wondering why the call to `update_config()` is needed?
  Why not simply assign the `filename` to both traits, like this:
  
      if os.path.isdir(f):
          self.notebook_dir = f
      elif os.path.isfile(f):
          self.file_to_run = f

To conclude the situation of the x9 `update_config()` sites with current *env-wins* defaults:
- x5 are fine;
- x2 need modifications whatever defaults are choosen - and most probably need other changes as well (i.e. for not duplicating stuff or even stop overriding methods);
- x1 suffers, but can retrofit not to depend on `config_update()` at all;
- x1 suffers (the "user-provided high priority calls"),

Anyhow, choosing current defaults is 4 vs 7 changes on downstream code.

> Apps sometimes store config as constants in the code. This is a violation of twelve-factor, which requires strict separation of config from code. Config varies substantially across deploys, code does not.

So the way that `StrictRedis` works is kind of magical. When you call `SetRedis.foo(...)` it then dispatches to `StrictRedis.execute_command("foo", ...)` by inspecting its own function name.

Therefore when you call a method like `get()` on the `StrictRedis` class, first it calls `get()`, then that immediately dispatches `execute_command()`, and then that has the bits that actually send data over the wire to redis and then waits for them to get back. That's why I'm doing the weird thing where I store the attributes in `_extra_tags` in the command method, and then use them in `execute_command`.

Custom C++ classes mapped to Python (``class_<>``, convenience wrappers around it like ``stl_binder.h``), Python type wrappers (``py::object, py::array``, etc.), and conversion type casters which convert C++ types into equivalent Python types and vice versa while creating copies in the process (for ``int``, ``std::string``, all stuff in ``stl.h``, ``eigen.h``). Authorization reuse is the biggest reason.

After successfully performing a DV challenge, the ACME server has a valid authorization object saying that it may issue a certificate to the owner of the account whose request created the authorization. This authorization does not expire immediately, even after the certificate issued. This means that the owner of an account key can issue certificates for that domain without performing DV challenges until the authorization expires. Because of this, if someone steals your account key, they can issue a certificate for your domain.

Let's Encrypt does this proactively, reusing authorizations even if you request a new one ([source](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/upcoming-change-valid-authz-reuse/16982)). This has lead to some [interesting conversations](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=12787735), but behavior like this is common practice for CAs and is completely valid in CAB forum requirements.

> 1. act like git pull (fetch + merge)

Would need to check out the branch first in that case (and there would have to
be a local branch). The current version does that, and then switches back to
the previous position. That feels a bit wonky though, but maybe it could work
alright in practice.

> 2. act like git fetch (fetch only)

Would want some way to rebase local work on top of new upstream stuff, so would
probably need additional commands in that case. Wonder if that might be too
complex.

> 3. scream and die unless a --rebase or --local-branch-strategy={rebase,detach,merge} or so flag was given
> 4. always fetch and detach HEAD (unless overridden by a flag)

IMO, it's nice if sync (by default at least) brings in upstream work while also
preserving local changes. I suspect that's what most people would expect.

Keeping a local branch gets a bit tricky when you can have SHAs. I wonder if
there might be gotchas related to changing the revision in the manifest file
too (e.g. to a different branch).

Always just doing a 'git rebase <revision>' is conceptually simplest at least
(and will work with a detached HEAD as well).

The implementation gets a bit tricky though, because e.g. 'git status' won't
know what the upstream is, and so won't tell you stuff like how many commits
behind you are. Gotta implement that stuff manually I think.
